item.title: ConnectNovu 黑客马拉松 2023 | ConnectNovu Hackathon 2023
item.description:
参与者使用电子邮件、短信、直接和推送等各种渠道使用 Novu 构建项目。

经过几个月的兴奋之后，结果出来了！

非常自豪地向 Novu 获奖者展示！

大家都想要的奖品🏆

有一些非常好的奖品！

但我想强调一下我们致力于的 Novu 社区大使奖励，而不仅仅是从我们的会员那里寄钱。

我们的团队聚集在葡萄牙的一家异地公司，我们都签署了获奖者的文件。

我希望这对你有意义！ 对我来说，这意味着很多！

制作者： Nova Tsui

原文:

ConnectNovu Hackathon was a global event focused on notifications.

Participants built projects with Novu using various channels like Email, SMS, Direct, and Push.

After very long months of excitement, the results are up!

Super proud to show Novu winners!

Prizes everybody wants 🏆

There are some really great prizes!

But I like to emphasize the Novu community ambassador reward, which we worked on, and not just sending money from our members.

Our team gathered in Portugal for a company offsite, and we have all signed the papers for the winners.

I hope it means something to you! For me, it means a lot!

#1 Place - Gptea

Made by: Nova Tsui

Gptea is an AI newsletter recommendation tailored to developers and startups using ChatGPT prompt.

Beautiful graphic! Very detailed code, and send newsletter notifications via Novu!

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/tsui66/connect-readme

#2 Place - Special Dayz

Made by: Ishaan Bedi

Special Dayz is a notification system that sends alerts for important dates, such as birthdays or anniversaries, with personalized content or gift recommendations. it sends you a Novu notification every time there is an important birthday. It can be yours or somebody else.

✅ Dark mode

✅ Glows

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/ishaanbedi/connect-readme/

#3 Place - Novu Donate

Made by: Anuforo Okechukwu Deede

Novu Donate is a charitable system that delivers updates on donations and volunteer opportunities.

Great design and a working flow 🚀

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/promiselxg/connect-readme/tr…

#4 Place - SentinelSky

Made by: Nikunj Navdiya

SentinelSky is a real-time web application that sends an email notification containing surrounding weather reports.

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/navdiya-nikunj/connect-readme

#5 Place - Reduced.to

Made by: Ori Granot

Reduced.to is a modern web application that reduces the length of the link URL. So it's easier to remember, share and track.

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/origranot/connect-readme

#6 Place - Management System for School

Made by: Abhishek Fadake

Building a notification system for schools that delivers updates and reminders to students and parents

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/fadkeabhi/School-Notificatio…

#7 Place - Mental health app

Made by: Lunar Marathon

Building a notification system for schools that delivers updates and reminders to students and parents

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/LunarMarathon/Millie-connect…

#8 Place - RemindX

Made by: Agu Bright Chidubem

An SMS notification system that sends reminders for upcoming appointments or events with customizable scheduling options.

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/LunarMarathon/Millie-connect…

#9 Place - Annapurna

Made by: Subhashis Paul

A push notification system that integrates with social impact organizations and sends alerts for volunteer opportunities or donation campaigns

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/subhashis2204/novu-project-a…

#10 Place - Crypto-alert

Made by: Emmanuel Kalu

Users can select a coin of their choice and set a target price to get a notification whenever the coin reaches the set target.

The available source code can be found here:

github.com/Emmancipated/crypto-alert

READY FOR THE NEXT HACKATHON?

(HackSquad is coming 😉)

item.title: 为什么GitHub不开源？ 与 Zach Holman（前 GitHub 和 GitLab） | Why is GitHub not open-source? with Zach Holman (ex GitHub & GitLab)
item.description:
然而，尽管 GitHub 致力于开源软件，但它本身并不是开源的。 在 GitHub 工作了五年、后来为 GitLab 提供咨询的工程师Zach Holman最近接受采访时，我们深入了解了 GitHub 成功的因素、其构建开发者社区的方法以及保持平台关闭的决定-来源。 让我们探讨一下这次对话的主要内容。

收听这个播客🎧
您可以在所有常规播客平台上收听开发者主导的这一集。

时机和建立开发者社区

在 GitHub 的成功中发挥关键作用的重要因素之一是时机。 通过吸引有影响力的开发人员的注意力并提供一个有价值的平台，GitHub 开始自然地获得关注。

文化和开发者布道

这种方法培育了一种协作和透明的文化，与世界各地的开发者产生了共鸣。 扎克·霍尔曼 (Zach Holman) 通过会议演讲和倡导开源工作方式在促进这种文化方面发挥了关键作用。

过渡到更大的组织

随着 GitHub 从一个小团队发展成为一家拥有 250 名员工的公司，它在维护其文化方面面临着独特的挑战。 管理人员的增加以及对更加结构化的流程的需求变得不可避免。 扎克承认，在保持生产力和创新的同时扩展公司文化是一项复杂的任务。 从无管理者的组织向层级结构的转变需要仔细的实施和调整。

开源困境

尽管 GitHub 专注于开源软件，但仍决定保持 GitHub 本身的闭源性，这是公司内部持续讨论的一个话题。 扎克·霍尔曼承认这两种方法的优点。 虽然产品的开源方面可以促进透明度和社区协作，但它也给管理贡献、处理潜在负面影响和保持竞争优势带来了挑战。 不过，他强调 GitHub 和 GitLab 都通过采用不同的方法取得了成功，这证明没有一条正确的道路。

从工程转向天使投资

他强调了投资科技公司时拥有技术知识的优势。 通过为不同的初创公司提供建议和投资，扎克获得了对不同行业的宝贵见解，拓宽了他的视野。 投资成为他顾问角色的延伸，使他能够利用自己的经验做出明智的决策。

包起来

对 Zach Holman 的采访提供了有关 GitHub 发展、扩展公司文化的挑战以及保持平台闭源背后的决策过程的宝贵见解。 虽然 GitHub 仍然是闭源的，但这次对话也强调了产品开源方面的优点。 最终，GitHub 和 GitLab 等平台的成功表明，不同的方法可以为开发者社区带来非凡的成就。



原文:

GitHub, a widely popular platform for developers, has revolutionized the way code is shared, collaborated on, and managed. However, despite its commitment to open-source software, GitHub itself is not open-source. In a recent interview with Zach Holman, an engineer who worked at GitHub for five years and later advised GitLab, we gain insights into the factors that contributed to GitHub's success, its approach to building a developer community, and the decision to keep the platform closed-source. Let's explore the key takeaways from this conversation.

Listen to this Podcast 🎧
You can listen to this episode of Developer-led on all regular podcast platforms.

Timing and Building a Developer Community

One of the significant factors that played a crucial role in GitHub's success was timing. Zach Holman emphasizes the importance of seizing the right moment to launch a product. GitHub's founders had a deep understanding of the Ruby community, which allowed them to build a strong initial user base. By capturing the attention of influential developers and providing a valuable platform, GitHub started gaining traction organically.

Culture and Developer Evangelism

Zach Holman sheds light on the role of culture and developer evangelism in GitHub's growth. GitHub embraced an open-source approach internally, modeling itself after successful open-source projects. This approach fostered a culture of collaboration and transparency, resonating with developers worldwide. Zach Holman played a pivotal role in promoting this culture through conference talks and advocating for the open-source way of working.

Transitioning to a Larger Organization

As GitHub grew from a small team to a company of 250 employees, it faced unique challenges in maintaining its culture. The addition of managers and the need for more structured processes became inevitable. Zach acknowledges that scaling the company culture while maintaining productivity and innovation was a complex task. The transition from a managerless organization to one with a hierarchical structure required careful implementation and adjustments.

The Open-Source Dilemma

The decision to keep GitHub itself closed-source, despite its focus on open-source software, was a subject of ongoing discussion within the company. Zach Holman acknowledges the merits of both approaches. While open-sourcing aspects of the product can promote transparency and community collaboration, it also presents challenges in managing contributions, dealing with potential negativity, and maintaining a competitive advantage. GitHub's experience and exposure to various scenarios influenced the team's perspective on open-sourcing their entire product.

GitHub vs. GitLab: Differences and Perspectives

Having also advised GitLab, another prominent platform for developers, Zach Holman provides insights into the significant differences between the two companies. GitLab's strong commitment to open-source principles and its transparent decision-making processes set it apart. Zach acknowledges that GitLab's open-sourceness has a significant impact on its culture and product development pace. However, he emphasizes that both GitHub and GitLab have found success by employing different approaches, proving that there isn't a single correct path.

Transitioning from Engineering to Angel Investing

Zach Holman's journey from engineering at GitHub to angel investing brings a unique perspective to the discussion. He highlights the advantage of having technical knowledge when investing in tech companies. By advising and investing in different startups, Zach gained valuable insights into diverse industries, broadening his perspective. Investing became an extension of his advisory role, allowing him to leverage his experience to make informed decisions.

Wrapping up

The interview with Zach Holman provides valuable insights into the growth of GitHub, the challenges of scaling company culture, and the decision-making process behind keeping the platform closed-source. GitHub's success can be attributed to factors such as perfect timing, a strong developer community, and an open-source culture. While GitHub remains closed-source, the conversation also highlights the merits of open-sourcing aspects of a product. Ultimately, the success of platforms like GitHub and GitLab showcases that different approaches can lead to remarkable achievements in the developer community.
item.author: jonathimer

item.title: 如何在技术面试中谈论您的开源经验 | How to Talk About Your Open Source Experience in a Tech Interview
item.description:
面试是一项可以加强的技能。 面试可能会令人沮丧、士气低落、随意、激烈，有时也很有趣。 他们可以让你怀疑自己，也可以验证你的技能。 这些采访中评估了很多东西，我个人知道，在这个过程中，我的紧张情绪占据了上风。 但就像任何其他技能一样，你了解的越多，练习的越多，你就会越好。 当你面试科技工作时，你会被问及你的经验。 对开源项目的贡献是展示您技能的好方法，因为它们可以展示您的技术技能、展示您的协作、解决问题的能力以及对工作的热情。 即使您作为贡献者拥有出色的经验，您也需要知道如何在技术面试中有效地谈论您的开源经验。 在本博客中，我们将探讨如何在技术面试中谈论您的开源经验。

有关如何通过开源找到工作的更多资源

突出相关性

在谈论您的开源经历时，请强调其与您正在面试的职位的相关性。 将您的贡献与该职位所需的技能联系起来。 讨论与公司技术堆栈或职位描述相符的开源社区的具体项目或方面。 这表明您有能力弥合开源与公司目标之间的差距。

响应示例 - 开发者倡导者角色

采访者：您能否举个例子，说明您与工程团队合作向社区成员提供教育内容，从而提高他们的技能和产品知识，同时促进宣传？

这个项目让我沉浸在这门语言中，并深入了解它的复杂性。

作为我处理的文档问题的一部分，我的主要职责是创建教育内容（包括教程），以帮助其他开发人员使用该语言。 我想确保社区拥有充分利用该语言功能所需的资源。

为了实现这一目标，我与工程团队和社区成员密切合作，以确定常见的痛点和需要进一步澄清的领域。 我积极参与论坛上的讨论并组织了专门针对贵公司技术堆栈的研讨会。

通过我的开源贡献，我不仅增强了我的语言技术专业知识，而且还培养了强大的沟通技巧。 我能够有效地向不同的受众阐明复杂的技术概念，培养他们的技能和产品的流畅性，同时倡导开源项目及其与开发人员社区的相关性。

描述您的贡献

首先解释您想要解决的问题或您正在开发的功能，为您的参与提供背景。 如果您面临挑战，请强调您是如何克服这些挑战的。 如果您知道您的贡献的影响，例如用户数量、积极反馈或其他开发人员的采用，也请分享此信息。 强调您在此过程中学到的经验教训以及它们如何塑造您的技术成长。

响应示例 - 前端开发人员

面试官：您能告诉我您所研究的问题或功能，为您的参与提供背景吗？

托马斯：当然！ 我最近参与的一个项目是一个名为“Contributions Connect”的开源 Web 应用程序。 它旨在连接当地社区并促进其成员之间的合作。 作为一名前端开发人员，我的职责是通过改进应用程序的 UI 和优化其性能来增强用户体验。

采访者：您在项目过程中遇到过什么挑战吗？ 你是如何克服它们的？

用户界面的初始版本在视觉上很吸引人，但缺乏必要的响应能力。 为了克服这个问题，我专注于识别瓶颈并实施性能优化​​，例如最小化 HTTP 请求、压缩资产和利用浏览器缓存。 通过实施这些技术，我显着提高了应用程序的整体加载时间和响应能力。

在开发 Contributions Connect 时，我积极与开源社区合作，收到了用户和其他开发人员的积极反馈和参与。 我们在 UI 和性能方面的改进不仅增强了用户体验，而且还提高了社区内其他开发人员的采用率。 从 GitHub 上该项目的分叉、星标和贡献数量的不断增加就可以看出这一点。

采访者：您从这次经历中学到了什么？它们如何影响您的技术成长？

我了解到，构建视觉上令人愉悦的用户界面只是等式的一部分； 确保最佳性能和速度同样重要。 通过这个项目，我对延迟加载、代码分割和缓存策略等性能优化技术有了深入的了解。 这些课程不仅增强了我的技术能力，还激发了我创建高效且可重用代码的热情。

展示协作和沟通技巧

开源项目本质上是协作的，因此展示您在团队中有效工作的能力非常重要。 谈论您如何与其他贡献者互动、如何分享想法以及如何解决冲突或意见分歧。 突出显示您在开源社区中担任的任何领导角色或职责，例如作为维护者或项目负责人。 强调您如何与项目社区进行有效的线上和线下沟通。

响应示例 - 项目经理

面试官：您能介绍一下您作为项目经理的经验和技能吗？

礼物：当然可以！ 当我从开发人员过渡到项目管理角色时，我在开源项目方面的经验为我提供了对有效项目管理实践的宝贵见解。 在我作为开发人员并过渡到项目管理期间，我有机会参与各种开源项目。 一次值得注意的经历是我参与了一个名为“AwesomeWebApp”的开源项目。 作为贡献者，我积极参与项目社区，与其他贡献者合作并分享想法以增强项目的功能。

在与其他贡献者的互动方面，我坚信要营造一个积极和包容的环境。 我定期参加在线讨论，为其他贡献者提供建设性的反馈和建议。 通过积极倾听不同的观点，我确保每个人的想法都得到考虑和尊重。 这种方法帮助我们保持了和谐高效的工作氛围。

为了解决冲突或意见分歧，我采用了外交和以解决方案为导向的方法。 当我们的团队在实施一项新功能时遇到分歧时，我想到了一个例子。 我发起了一次讨论，以了解不同的观点，鼓励公开对话和相互理解。 通过有效的沟通和妥协，我们达成了各方都满意的共识。

在领导角色方面，我承担了“AwesomeWebApp”项目的项目维护人员的责任。 作为维护者，我监督项目的进展，审查和合并贡献，并促进贡献者之间的讨论。 这段经历为我提供了协调工作和确保项目整体成功的宝贵见解。

当谈到项目社区内部的沟通时，我认识到在线上和线下保持牢固联系的重要性。 我积极参与在线论坛，及时更新和澄清与项目相关的疑问。 此外，我组织并参加了聚会和会议，在那里我有机会与其他贡献者面对面互动，促进个人联系并加强项目的社区精神。

总的来说，我作为开源项目开发人员的经验使我具备了团队合作、沟通、解决冲突和领导力方面的基本技能。 我相信这些经验为我过渡到项目管理奠定了坚实的基础，因为它们磨练了我在不同团队中有效工作以及与各个级别的利益相关者互动的能力。

讨论您的学习经历

开源项目为持续学习和技能发展提供了绝佳的平台。 分享您的开源经验如何扩展您的技术知识并磨练您解决问题的能力。 讨论您在贡献过程中遇到的新编程语言、框架或工具。 提及您收到的任何反馈或代码审查以及它们如何帮助您提高开发人员的水平。 展现您对学习和成长的热情。

响应示例 - 全栈工程师

面试官：请告诉我们您的开源经历以及它如何扩展您的技术知识并磨练您解决问题的能力。 具体来说，您能否分享一个您利用全栈技能的项目或问题的示例？

利用我的全栈技能的一个具体例子是当我在一个名为 XYZ 的流行开源项目中解决一个问题时。

该问题涉及通过实施缓存机制来优化 RESTful API 端点的性能。 作为一名后端开发人员，我了解高效数据检索和传输的重要性，但这一挑战也让我能够探索前端。 我需要评估当前的缓存策略并提出改进建议以增强整体用户体验。

为了解决这个问题，我首先熟悉了该项目的代码库及其使用的技术堆栈。 它涉及在后端使用 Node.js 和在前端使用 React.js 的组合。 尽管我的专业经验主要集中在后端开发，但我渴望扩展我的技能并接受这一挑战的全栈性质。

我深入研究了现有代码，确定了需要优化的区域，并研究了适合此场景的不同缓存技术。 利用我的后端知识，我建议使用 Redis 实现服务器端缓存，并通过使用记忆技术实现客户端缓存的组合。 我与前端团队合作，确保将这些缓存机制集成到 API 响应处理和 UI 组件中。

在整个过程中，我积极寻求反馈并与开源社区的其他贡献者一起进行代码审查。 他们的见解和建议不仅帮助我提高了代码质量，而且拓宽了我对全栈开发最佳实践的理解。 他们的专业知识指导我解决边缘情况并进一步优化我的实施。

这段经历展示了我作为开发人员学习和成长的热情。 它展示了我利用我的后端专业知识来适应新的编程语言、框架和工具的能力，同时在完整的堆栈上下文中承担前端职责。 解决这个开源问题让我扩大了我的技术视野，增强了我解决问题的能力，并坚定了我追求全栈角色的愿望。

谈论开源社区

开源社区因协作、知识共享和回馈而蓬勃发展。 谈论您在编码之外对社区的参与，例如参与讨论、指导新人或贡献文档。 讨论开源社区如何影响您的职业发展以及您如何为其发展做出贡献。 展示您对开源的热情以及您对对开发者社区产生积极影响的奉献精神。

响应示例 - 高级软件工程师

面试官：您能否提供一个领导经验的例子来证明您有能力指导和指导团队中的初级开发人员？

詹姆斯：我相信领导力不仅仅是技术技能，还包括激励和引导他人成长和成功的能力。 我想起了一次特殊的经历，我有机会通过初级开发人员的开源经验来指导和支持他们。

在我之前的公司，我们正在开发一个开源项目，旨在简化基于云的应用程序的部署流程。 作为一名高级软件工程师，我积极参与开源社区，参与讨论，贡献文档，并指导想要参与的新人。

我意识到，指导早期职业人士不仅要分享技术知识，还要创造一个支持性的环境，让他们能够轻松地提出问题和探索自己的想法。 为了促进这一点，我定期组织虚拟聚会，初级开发人员可以在其中分享他们的进展，讨论挑战并寻求指导。

在其中一次聚会中，一位初级开发人员正在努力解决特定功能的实现问题。 我不是简单地提供解决方案，而是鼓励他们批判性思考，指导他们分解问题，并提出潜在的方法。 我们一起探索不同的选择，讨论权衡，并最终得出一个优雅的解决方案。 见证他们的成长、信心的增强是一次令人欣慰的经历。

为了确保持续的学习体验，我还进行了代码审查，提供建设性的反馈，帮助初级开发人员改进他们的编码实践并更深入地了解软件开发原理。 我相信以身作则，因此我确保自己贡献高质量的代码并遵循最佳实践，设定标准。

通过这次经历，我意识到指导可以对接受指导的个人和整个社区产生巨大的影响。 这不仅仅是帮助某人编写代码；而是帮助他们。 这是为了让他们成为更好的开发人员并培养知识共享和协作的文化。

我还积极鼓励初级开发人员通过承担较小的任务、审查拉取请求并提出改进建议来为开源项目做出贡献。 通过这样做，他们不仅发展了自己的技术技能，还获得了主人翁意识和对自己的贡献感到自豪。

其他注意事项：准备好展示您的代码

一些技术面试可能会要求您通过分享开源贡献的链接来展示您的编码技能。 准备好展示您的代码或其他贡献，并解释您的决定背后的理由。 练习简洁地解释并准备好回答与您所处理的代码库相关的问题。 引导招聘经理做出您最引以为傲的贡献的一种好方法是将其发送到您的亮点页面。

对于我们大多数人来说，要想做好面试需要练习和经验。 通过强调您的贡献的相关性、描述您的成就、展示协作和沟通技巧、讨论您的学习经历以及强调您对开源社区的参与，您应该能够给面试官留下深刻的印象。 请记住，这是一个分享您的热情并展示您在开源之旅中学到的所有知识的绝佳机会。 祝你好运！



原文:

Interviewing is a skill that can be strengthened. Interviews can be frustrating, demoralizing, casual, intense, and occasionally fun. They can make you doubt yourself or they can validate your skillset. There’s a lot being evaluated in these interviews and I know, personally, that nerves have gotten the better of me in that process. But just like any other skill, the more you learn about how to do it, the more you practice, the better you get. When you’re interviewing for a tech job, you’re going to be asked about your experience. Contributions to open source projects are a great way to demonstrate your skills because they can demonstrate your technical skills, showcase your collaboration, problem-solving abilities, and passion for your work. Even if you’ve had a stellar experience as a contributor, you need to know how to effectively talk about your open source experience during the tech interview. In this blog, we’ll explore how to talk about your open source experience in a tech interview.

More Resources on How to Land a Job with Open Source

Highlight the Relevance

When talking about your open source experience, emphasize its relevance to the position you're interviewing for. Make a connection between your contributions and the skills required for the role. Discuss specific projects or aspects of the open source community that align with the company's tech stack or the job description. This demonstrates your ability to bridge the gap between open source and the company's goals.

Example Response - Developer Advocate Role

Interviewer: Can you provide an example of a time when you partnered with an engineering team to deliver educational content to community members, leading to enhancing their skills and product knowledge while also promoting advocacy?

Mia: One significant open source project I contributed to was a developer toolkit for a programming language that closely resembles the tech stack utilized by your company. This project allowed me to immerse myself in the language and gain an in-depth understanding of its intricacies.

As part of the documentation issue that I worked on, my main responsibility was to create educational content, including tutorials, to empower fellow developers using the language. I wanted to ensure that the community had the resources they needed to fully leverage the language's capabilities.

To achieve this, I collaborated closely with the engineering team and community members to identify common pain points and areas that required further clarification. I actively engaged in discussions on forums and organized workshops that specifically focused on your company's technology stack.

Through my open source contributions, I not only enhanced my technical expertise in the language but also developed strong communication skills. I was able to effectively articulate complex technical concepts to a diverse audience, building their skills and product fluency while advocating for the open source project and its relevance to the developer community.

Describe Your Contributions

Start by explaining the problem you aimed to solve or the feature you worked on, providing context for your involvement. If you faced challenges, highlight how you overcame them. If you know the impact of your contributions, such as the number of users, positive feedback, or adoption by other developers, share this information as well. Emphasize the lessons you learned during the process and how they have shaped your technical growth.

Example Response - Front-End Developer

Interviewer: Can you tell me about a problem or feature you worked on, providing context for your involvement?

Tomás: Absolutely! One project I recently contributed to was an open source web application called "Contributions Connect." It aimed to connect local communities and facilitate collaboration among their members. As a frontend developer, my role was to enhance the user experience by improving the application's UI and optimizing its performance.

Interviewer: Did you face any challenges during the project? How did you overcome them?

Tomás: One of the main challenges I encountered was optimizing the application's performance and speed on the front end. The initial version of the UI was visually appealing but lacked the necessary responsiveness. To overcome this, I focused on identifying bottlenecks and implementing performance optimizations, such as minimizing HTTP requests, compressing assets, and leveraging browser caching. By implementing these techniques, I significantly improved the overall loading time and responsiveness of the application.

While working on Contributions Connect, I actively collaborated with the open source community, receiving positive feedback and engagement from both users and fellow developers. Our improvements in UI and performance not only led to an enhanced user experience but also resulted in increased adoption by other developers within the community. This was evident through the growing number of forks, stars, and contributions to the project on GitHub.

Interviewer: What lessons did you learn from this experience and how have they shaped your technical growth?

Tomás: This experience taught me the importance of striking a balance between aesthetics and performance in frontend development. I learned that building a visually pleasing UI is only part of the equation; ensuring optimal performance and speed is equally crucial. Through this project, I developed a deep understanding of performance optimization techniques, such as lazy loading, code splitting, and caching strategies. These lessons have not only enhanced my technical abilities but also instilled in me a passion for creating efficient and reusable code.

Showcase Collaboration and Communication Skills

Open source projects are collaborative by nature, so it's important to showcase your ability to work effectively in a team. Talk about how you interacted with other contributors, how you shared ideas, and how you resolved conflicts or differences of opinion. Highlight any leadership roles or responsibilities you had within the open source community, such as being a maintainer or a project lead. Emphasize how you effectively communicated with the project community, both online and offline.

Example Response - Project Manager

Interviewer: Can you tell me about your experience and skills as a project manager?

Gift: Certainly! While I'm transitioning from a developer to a project management role, my experience with open source projects has provided me with valuable insights into effective project management practices. During my time as a developer and transitioning into project management, I had the opportunity to work on various open source projects. One notable experience was my involvement with an open source project called "AwesomeWebApp." As a contributor, I actively engaged with the project community, collaborating with other contributors and sharing ideas to enhance the project's functionality.

In terms of interacting with other contributors, I firmly believe in fostering a positive and inclusive environment. I regularly participated in online discussions, providing constructive feedback and suggestions to fellow contributors. By actively listening to different perspectives, I ensured that everyone's ideas were considered and respected. This approach helped us maintain a harmonious and productive work atmosphere.

To resolve conflicts or differences of opinion, I employed a diplomatic and solution-oriented approach. One instance comes to mind when our team encountered a disagreement regarding the implementation of a new feature. I initiated a discussion to understand the varying viewpoints, encouraging open dialogue and mutual understanding. Through effective communication and compromise, we were able to reach a consensus that satisfied all parties involved.

In terms of leadership roles, I took on the responsibility of being a project maintainer for the "AwesomeWebApp" project. As a maintainer, I oversaw the project's progress, reviewed and merged contributions, and facilitated discussions among contributors. This experience provided me with valuable insights into coordinating efforts and ensuring the project's overall success.

When it comes to communication within the project community, I recognized the importance of maintaining strong connections both online and offline. I actively participated in online forums, providing timely updates and clarifications to project-related queries. Additionally, I organized and attended meetups and conferences, where I had the opportunity to interact with fellow contributors face-to-face, fostering personal connections and strengthening the project's community spirit.

Overall, my experience as a developer working on open source projects has equipped me with essential skills in teamwork, communication, conflict resolution, and leadership. I believe these experiences have laid a solid foundation for my transition into project management, as they have honed my abilities to work effectively within diverse teams and engage with stakeholders at various levels.

Discuss Your Learning Experience

Open source projects provide an excellent platform for continuous learning and skill development. Share how your open source experience has expanded your technical knowledge and honed your problem-solving abilities. Discuss the new programming languages, frameworks, or tools you encountered during your contributions. Mention any feedback or code reviews you received and how they helped you improve as a developer. Demonstrate your enthusiasm for learning and growth.

Example Response - Full Stack Engineer

Interviewer: Tell us about your open source experience and how it has expanded your technical knowledge and honed your problem-solving abilities. Specifically, could you share an example of a project or issue where you utilized your full stack skills?

Mari: During my open source experience, I've had the opportunity to dive into various projects that have expanded my technical knowledge and sharpened my problem-solving abilities. One specific example of utilizing my full stack skills was when I took on an issue in a popular open source project called XYZ.

The issue involved optimizing the performance of a RESTful API endpoint by implementing caching mechanisms. As a backend developer, I understood the importance of efficient data retrieval and transmission, but this challenge allowed me to explore the frontend side as well. I needed to assess the current caching strategy and propose improvements to enhance the overall user experience.

To tackle this issue, I first familiarized myself with the project's codebase and the technology stack it utilized. It involved working with a combination of Node.js on the backend and React.js on the frontend. Although my professional experience was primarily focused on backend development, I was eager to expand my skill set and embrace the full stack nature of this challenge.

I dove into the existing code, identified the areas that required optimization, and researched different caching techniques suitable for this scenario. Leveraging my backend knowledge, I suggested implementing a combination of server-side caching using Redis and client-side caching through the use of memoization techniques. I collaborated with the frontend team to ensure the integration of these caching mechanisms in the API response handling and UI components.

Throughout this process, I actively sought feedback and engaged in code reviews with other contributors in the open source community. Their insights and suggestions not only helped me improve my code quality but also broadened my understanding of best practices in full stack development. Their expertise guided me in addressing edge cases and optimizing my implementation further.

This experience demonstrated my enthusiasm for learning and growth as a developer. It showcased my ability to adapt to new programming languages, frameworks, and tools by utilizing my backend expertise while embracing the frontend responsibilities in a full stack context. Working on this open source issue allowed me to expand my technical horizons, reinforce my problem-solving skills, and solidify my desire to pursue a full stack role.

Talk About the Open Source Community

The open source community thrives on collaboration, knowledge sharing, and giving back. Talk about your involvement in the community beyond coding, such as participating in discussions, mentoring newcomers, or contributing to documentation. Discuss how the open source community has influenced your professional development and how you have contributed to its growth. Showcase your passion for open source and your dedication to making a positive impact on the developer community.

Example Response - Senior Software Engineer

Interviewer: Can you provide an example of a leadership experience that demonstrates your ability to mentor and guide junior developers your team?

James: I believe that leadership goes beyond just technical skills and encompasses the ability to inspire and guide others towards their growth and success. One particular experience comes to mind where I had the opportunity to mentor and support junior developers through their open source experience.

At my previous company, we were working on an open source project aimed at simplifying deployment processes for cloud-based applications. As a Senior Software Engineer, I actively participated in the open source community, engaging in discussions, contributing to documentation, and mentoring newcomers who wanted to get involved.

I realized that mentoring early career folks was not just about sharing technical knowledge but also about creating a supportive environment where they felt comfortable asking questions and exploring their ideas. To facilitate this, I organized regular virtual meetups where junior developers could share their progress, discuss challenges, and seek guidance.

During one of these meetups, a junior developer was struggling with a particular feature implementation. Instead of simply providing a solution, I encouraged them to think critically, guided them in breaking down the problem, and suggested potential approaches. Together, we explored different options, discussed trade-offs, and eventually arrived at an elegant solution. It was a gratifying experience to witness their growth and see their confidence increase.

To ensure a continuous learning experience, I also conducted code reviews, providing constructive feedback to help junior developers improve their coding practices and gain a deeper understanding of software development principles. I believe in leading by example, so I made sure to contribute high-quality code and follow best practices myself, setting a standard.

Through this experience, I realized the immense impact mentoring can have on both the individual being mentored and the community as a whole. It's not just about helping someone with their code; it's about empowering them to become better developers and fostering a culture of knowledge-sharing and collaboration.

I also actively encouraged the junior developers to contribute back to the open source project by taking ownership of smaller tasks, reviewing pull requests, and suggesting improvements. By doing so, they not only developed their technical skills but also gained a sense of ownership and pride in their contributions.

Additional Consideration: Be Prepared to Show Your Code

Some tech interviews may require you to showcase your coding skills by sharing links to your open source contributions. Be ready to present your code or other contributions and explain the rationale behind your decisions. Practice explaining concisely and be prepared to answer questions related to the codebase you worked on. One great way to lead the hiring manager to the contributions you’re most proud of is to send them to your highlights page.

For most of us, it takes practice and experience to interview well. By highlighting the relevance of your contributions, describing your accomplishments, showcasing collaboration and communication skills, discussing your learning experience, and emphasizing your involvement in the open source community, you should be able to create an impression that interviewers will remember. And remember, this is a great opportunity to share your passion and demonstrate all the things you’ve learned through your open source journey. Good luck!
item.author: bekahhw

item.title: 横切面和操作员交互 | Crossplane and operators interactions
item.description:
在这篇关于 Kubernetes 运算符的新文章中，我们将讨论一个已经存在的运算符： Crossplane 。

什么是交叉平面？

这些云资源可以来自不同的云提供商，例如 AWS、GCP...

这意味着您可以从 Kubernetes 集群中动态地在云中创建资源。

以下示例将在 AWS 中创建一个 S3 存储桶。

 apiVersion: s3.aws.upbound.io/v1beta1 kind: Bucket metadata: generateName: crossplane-bucket- labels: docs.crossplane.io/example: provider-aws spec: forProvider: region: us-east-2 providerConfigRef: name: default

这太疯狂了，不是吗？

如何安装十字平面？

如果您有兴趣在集群上使用 Crossplane 并使用它，您可以检查以下链接，其中对每个云提供商都有很好的解释：

  • 使用其他运营商的 Crossplane！？

    当我们在 Kubernetes 集群中安装 Crossplane 时，其所有自定义资源现在都可以在集群中使用。 所以我们的操作员可以使用它们来管理新的资源实例。

    在本系列之前的文章中，我们提到可以使用 Operator 来管理 Grafana、Prometheus、Postgres 和 OpenTelemetry 部署。 在我们的新上下文中，由于前 3 个在 AWS 中可用，这意味着我们的操作员可以在 AWS 中动态创建这些资源！

    下载库

    在我们的操作员中要做的第一件事是下载 crossplane 库。

     go install github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws go install github.com/crossplane/crossplane-runtime

    它们将使我们能够访问所有常见的 Crossplane 资源以及AWS 的所有特定资源。

    管理模式

    在main.go文件中，有一个init函数，我们到现在为止都忽略了。

     func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) }

    像这样声明，它将通过AddToScheme方法向操作员提供对所有基本 Kubernetes 资源的访问权限。

    因此，如果我们希望能够使用 Crossplane 中的自定义资源，我们必须添加另一个声明。

     import ( crossplane "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis" ) func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) utilruntime.Must(crossplane.AddToScheme(scheme)) }

    始终使用相同的模式，您需要导入包含您想要使用的所有资源的库的AddToScheme函数，以及init函数中的所有资源。

    这是非常重要的事情，不要忘记！ 添加您需要的所有注释，以便为您的操作员提供新资源的正确权限。

    使用新资源

    现在我们已经完成了能够使用操作员提供的 Crossplane 资源的所有设置，我们可以在 Reconcile 方法中使用它们！

    例子

    import ( "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis/s3/v1beta1" ) ... myBucket := &v1beta1.Bucket{} // Refers to an AWS S3 Bucket err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myBucket) ...

    概括

    我们在这篇文章中发现了 Crossplane，并发现它可以在操作员之间进行交互。
    而且有很多运营商！ 例如Datadog 、 Grafana 、 Kong或Litmus Chaos 。

    如果您想查找运营商列表，请查看Operatorhub.io 。

    此外，根据我们今天所看到的情况，我们现在能够创建像微服务这样的操作符，每个操作符都有一个重点范围，并使事情更容易开发和扩展。 所以想想所有可以完全自动化完成的事情！

    谈到自动化，在本系列的下一篇文章中，我们将讨论Crossplane 与 Terraform 。

    我希望它能对您有所帮助，如果您有任何疑问（不存在愚蠢的问题）或有些问题您不清楚，请随时在评论中添加您的问题或直接在LinkedIn上与我联系。



原文:

In this new post about Kubernetes operators, we will talk about an operator which already exist : Crossplane.

What is Crossplane?

Crossplane is a Kubernetes operator which allows you to manage cloud resources from Kubernetes. These cloud resources can come from differents cloud providers like AWS, GCP...

It means that from your Kubernetes cluster, you can dynamically create resources in a cloud.

The following example will create a S3 bucket in AWS.

apiVersion: s3.aws.upbound.io/v1beta1 kind: Bucket metadata: generateName: crossplane-bucket- labels: docs.crossplane.io/example: provider-aws spec: forProvider: region: us-east-2 providerConfigRef: name: default

This is crazy, isn't it?

How to install Crossplane?

If you are interested by using Crossplane on your cluster and using it, you can check the following links where everything is well explained for each cloud provider:

  • Setup for AWS
  • Setup for Azure
  • Setup for GCP

Using Crossplane from another operator!?

As we installed Crossplane in our Kubernetes cluster, all its custom resources are now available in the cluster. So our operator can use them to manage new resource instances.

In previous posts of this serie, we mentionned that we can use an operator to manage Grafana, Prometheus, Postgres and OpenTelemetry deployments. With our new context, as the 3 first are available in AWS, it means that our operator can dynamically create these resources in AWS!

*Note: For all the following examples, I will only talk about AWS but it will be similar for other cloud providers.

Download libraries

The first thing to do in our operator is to download crossplane librairies.

go install github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws go install github.com/crossplane/crossplane-runtime

They will allow us to access to all the common Crossplane resources and all the specific resources for AWS.

Manage schemas

In the main.go file, there is a init function that we ignore until now.

func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) }

Declared like this, it will give access to the operator to all the basic Kubernetes resources with the method AddToScheme.

So if we want to be able to use custom resources from Crossplane, we must add another declaration.

import ( crossplane "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis"
) func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) utilruntime.Must(crossplane.AddToScheme(scheme))
}

Always working with the same pattern, you need to import the AddToScheme function of the librairie containing all the resources you want to work with, and all it in the init function.

RBAC

It's something really important to not forget! Go add all the annotations you need to give the correct rights to your operator on the new resources.

Using new resources

Now that we have everything setup to be able to use the Crossplane resources from our operator, we can use them in our Reconcile method!

Example

import ( "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis/s3/v1beta1"
) ... myBucket := &v1beta1.Bucket{} // Refers to an AWS S3 Bucket err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myBucket)
...

Summary

We discovered Crossplane in this post and saw that it's possible to make interactions between operators.
And there are a LOT of operators! Datadog, Grafana, Kong or Litmus Chaos for example.

Check Operatorhub.io if you want to find a list of operators.

Also, with what we saw today, we are now able to create operators like microservices, with each of them have a focused scope and make things easier to develop and scale. So think about all the things which can be done in full automation!

Talking about automation, in the next post of this serie, we will talk about Crossplane vs Terraform.

I hope it will help you and if you have any questions (there are not dumb questions) or some points are not clear for you, don't hesitate to add your question in the comments or to contact me directly on LinkedIn.
item.author: mxglt

item.title: 交叉平面或组合操作员 | Crossplane ou la combination d'opérateurs
item.description:
这是 Kubernetes 操作员系列的新剧集，讲述了一个类似存在的操作员： Crossplane 。

代表 Kubernetes 集群，您可以通过云提供商获取资源。

在 AWS 上创建 S3 存储桶的示例

apiVersion: s3.aws.upbound.io/v1beta1 kind: Bucket metadata: generateName: crossplane-bucket- labels: docs.crossplane.io/example: provider-aws spec: forProvider: region: us-east-2 providerConfigRef: name: default

不存在偏差的可能性吗？

评论 l'安装程序？

如果您需要安装 Crossplane sur votre cluster et l'utiliser，请注意以下事项，请参阅文档：

  • 使用跨平面来操作其他操作员吗？

    在 Kubernetes 集群上使用 Crossplane 安装程序，可以使用集群上的个人资源。 因此，我们将创建/管理您的资源。

    在该系列的先行文章中，提到了我们可以轻松管理 Grafana、Prometheus、Postgres 和 OpenTelemetry 的操作。 注意：对于后续示例，我无法与 AWS 合作，但对于其他云提供商来说，可能是不负责任的。

    图书馆电话充电

    首映选择了 les librairies crossplane dans votre projet 的公平收费

    go install github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws go install github.com/crossplane/crossplane-runtime

    您可以访问Crossplane 公共资源集合，以获取AWS 的特定资源。

    盖勒图式

    在main.go文件中，您可以忽略当前的功能init声明。

     func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) }

    声明（en simplifiant vulgairement） ，您可以通过AddToScheme方法访问 Kubernetes 基础资源集合。

    因此，如果您将资源添加到基础组件中，则将成为新的声明。

     import ( crossplane "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis" ) func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) utilruntime.Must(crossplane.AddToScheme(scheme)) }

    如果使用 même 格式，则可以使用AddToScheme方法导入库内容，以获取内部资源和应用程序的功能init 。

    这是最重要的选择！ 所有注释都不要用于自动操作和利用新资源。

    新兴资源的利用

    维护你所有的一切，你都可以使用新的资源，你可以使用这些资源！

    示例

    import ( "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis/s3/v1beta1" ) ... myBucket := &v1beta1.Bucket{} err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myBucket) ...

    结论

    在这一篇文章的最后一篇中，我们讨论了与 Crossplane 示例相结合的操作者的可能性。 如果您愿意，请关注Operatorhub.io 。

    最后，开发者需要选择更合适的设施、微服务的基本范围。 想象一下自动化的可能性！

    另外，我们将讨论Crossplane 与 Terraform之间的一系列问题。

    J'espère que ça vous aidera et si jamais vous avez des questions, quelles qu'elles soient (Il n'y a jamais de questions bêtes!) ou des points qui ne sont pas clairs pour vous, n'hésitez pas à laisser un message dans les commentaires ou à me joindre directement sur LinkedIn (même pour parler d'autres sujets!) . J'espère que ça vous aidera et si jamais vous avez des questions, quelles qu'elles soient (Il n'y a jamais de questions bêtes!) ou des point qui ne sont pas clairs pour vous, n'hésitez pas à laisser un消息 dans les commentaires ou à me joindre directement sur LinkedIn (même pour parler d'autres sujets!) 。



原文:

Dans ce nouvel épisode de la série sur les opérateurs Kubernetes, on va parler d'un opérateur qui existe déjà : Crossplane.

Qu'est-ce que Crossplane ?

Crossplane est un opérateur Kubernetes qui permet de gérer depuis Kubernetes des ressources dans différents cloud provider. (AWS, GCP ...)

Ca signifie que depuis votre cluster Kubernetes, vous pouvez créer des ressources dans un cloud Provider.

L'exemple suivant permet de créer un bucket S3 dans AWS

apiVersion: s3.aws.upbound.io/v1beta1 kind: Bucket metadata: generateName: crossplane-bucket- labels: docs.crossplane.io/example: provider-aws spec: forProvider: region: us-east-2 providerConfigRef: name: default

Les possibilités deviennent dingues non?

Comment l'installer?

Si vous êtes intéressés pour installer Crossplane sur votre cluster et l'utiliser, je vous invite à regarder les liens suivants, la documentation est très bien :

  • Setup pour AWS
  • Setup pour Azure
  • Setup pour GCP

Utiliser crossplane depuis un autre opérateur?

Vu qu'on peut installer Crossplane sur notre cluster Kubernetes, toutes ses ressources personnalisées sont donc disponibles sur le cluster. Par conséquent votre opérateur peut gérer la création/mise à jour de ces ressources.

Dans les articles précédents de cette série, on a mentionné le fait qu'un opérateur pouvait nous permettre de faciliter la gestion de déploiements Grafana, Prometheus, Postgres et OpenTelemetry. Sachant que les 3 premiers font partis des services mis à disposition dans AWS, ça veut dire que dans votre opérateur vous pourriez créer dynamiquement les ressources pour vos clients dans AWS !

Note: Pour l'ensemble des exemples suivants, je ne parlerais que d'AWS, mais tout est disponible pour les autres cloud providers.

Télécharger les librairies

La première chose à faire est de télécharger les librairies crossplane dans votre projet

go install github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws go install github.com/crossplane/crossplane-runtime

Elles vont vous permettre d'accéder à l'ensemble des ressources communes de Crossplane, ainsi qu'à toutes les ressources spécifiques à AWS.

Gérer les schémas

Dans le fichier main.go, il y a la déclaration de la fonction init que l'on a pas mal ignoré jusqu'à présent.

func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) }

Déclarée ainsi (en simplifiant vulgairement), elle donne accès à l'opérateur à l'ensemble des ressources de bases Kubernetes via la méthode AddToScheme.

Par conséquent, si on veut accéder à des ressources qui ne font pas partie des éléments de base, il faut ajouter une nouvelle déclaration.

import ( crossplane "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis"
) func init() { utilruntime.Must(clientgoscheme.AddToScheme(scheme)) utilruntime.Must(crossplane.AddToScheme(scheme))
}

Fonctionnant toujours avec le même format, il faut aller importer la méthode AddToScheme de la librairie contenant les ressources qui vous intéresse et l'appeler dans la fonction init.

RBAC

C'est quelque chose d'important à ne pas oublier! Aller ajouter les annotations dont vous avez besoin pour autoriser votre opérateur à utiliser ces nouvelles ressources.

Utilisation des nouvelles ressources

Maintenant que vous avez fait tout ce qu'il fallait pour permettre à votre opérateur d'utiliser ces nouvelles ressources, vous pouvez les utiliser dans vos traîtements!

Exemple

import ( "github.com/crossplane-contrib/provider-aws/apis/s3/v1beta1"
) ... myBucket := &v1beta1.Bucket{} err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, myBucket)
...

Conclusion

Au final dans cet article, on aura vu qu'il était possible de combiner des opérateurs avec l'exemple de Crossplane. Et des opérateurs il en existe beaucoup! Datadog, Grafana, Kong ou encore Litmus Chaos pour ne citer que ceux-là!

Si vous en cherchez, le meilleur endroit à regarder est Operatorhub.io.

Mais au final cette imbrication d'opérateurs permet de développer beaucoup de choses bien plus facilements, tels des microservices qui ont chacun un scope précis. Donc imaginez tout ce qui peut être possible au niveau de l'automatisation!

Et justement, on va parler de ça dans la dernière partie de cette série où on va parler de Crossplane vs Terraform.

J'espère que ça vous aidera et si jamais vous avez des questions, quelles qu'elles soient (Il n'y a jamais de questions bêtes!) ou des points qui ne sont pas clairs pour vous, n'hésitez pas à laisser un message dans les commentaires ou à me joindre directement sur LinkedIn (même pour parler d'autres sujets!).
item.author: mxglt

item.title: Html Canvas 中的 Perfboard 设计器 | Perfboard Designer in Html Canvas
item.description:
如果您是像我一样的电子爱好者，您可能遇到过一个常见问题。 设计电路并在面包板上进行测试后，您需要一种更可靠的方式来存储它。 这就是穿孔板的用武之地。它们为创建坚固的电路提供了一种经济实惠的选择。

穿孔板有孔，您可以在其中放置组件，然后焊接它们之间的连接。 简单的电路很容易处理，但复杂的电路和有限的空间会让事情变得棘手。

确保正确的连接变得至关重要，而在旅途中这样做可能会导致混乱的结果或耗尽空间。 虽然有一些程序可用于设计 PCB/穿孔板，但它们并不适合初学者。 我尝试了一些，但它们并不能满足只想设计预制板的爱好者。

然后我遇到了 Tinkercad，一个更适合初学者的设计师。 然而，作为一名具有基本 HTML 画布知识的程序员，我想到创建自己的解决方案。 这有多难，对吧？

对于我的初始原型，我从纯 HTML 和 JavaScript 开始。 随着项目变得越来越复杂，我转而使用 Vite 和 TypeScript。 如果项目继续扩展，我可能会考虑迁移到更高级的框架，如 React 或 Vue.js。 但现在，我的重点是创建一个可用的画布。

嘿 ChatGPT，你能帮我吗？

我联系了 ChatGPT 来帮助我开始。 经过几次迭代，它提供了一个不错的起始代码，为我节省了大约 4 个小时。 然而，随着事情变得越来越复杂，提示变得具有挑战性，我决定坚持使用我的代码。 我自己重构并继续开发。

我介绍了修复和新功能，例如悬停效果和根据用户输入扩展点网格。 但随着纯 HTML 和 JavaScript 代码变得混乱且难以阅读，我决定用 TypeScript 重写它并分离功能。 我使用 Vite 构建了 TypeScript 代码并对其进行了重构，从而得到了更简单、更灵活的代码。

目前的特点：

在短短一个周末的时间里，我的目标是创建一款可用的最小可行产品 (MVP)，并实现了以下功能：

  • 调整点网格的大小
  • 点
    • 添加颜色
    • 添加描述/删除
    • 移动点
    • 选择点
    • 悬停点 - 添加效果，显示整个描述
  • 线路
    • 添加颜色
    • 连接两个点
    • 删除行
    • 选择线路
    • 悬停效果
  • 将项目另存为图像
  • 将项目保存到文件以供共享或将来使用
  • 从本地存储保存和加载项目
  • 重置项目
  • 撤消/重做更改
  • 键盘快捷键

查看GitHub 代码库或现场演示

解决问题：

选择正确的项目来启动可能具有挑战性。 最好的学习项目是解决你自己的问题的项目。 从事您感兴趣并满足您需求的事情会更令人愉快和满足。

未来的计划：

  • 创建更加用户友好的工具菜单
  • 完成IC功能
  • 添加组件创建者
  • 迁移到 React 或 Vue

贡献：

我对 CSS 不是特别熟练，所以如果您愿意做出贡献（例如工具菜单设计），我们将非常感谢您的帮助。

在以后的文章中，我可能会深入研究底层的数学和解决方案。 感谢您的阅读，祝您有美好的一天！ ❤️ ❤️



原文:

If you're an electronics hobbyist like me, you might have faced a common problem. After designing a circuit and testing it on a breadboard, you want a more reliable way to store it. That's where perfboards come in. They provide a budget-friendly option for creating robust circuits.

Perfboards have holes where you can place your components and then solder the connections between them. Simple circuits are easy to handle, but things get tricky with complex circuits and limited space.

Ensuring the right connections becomes crucial, and doing it on the go can lead to a messy outcome or running out of space. While there are programs available for designing PCB / perfboards, they aren't beginner-friendly. I tried a few, but they didn't cater to hobbyists who simply wanted to design prefboards.

Then I came across Tinkercad, a more beginner-friendly designer. However, being a programmer with basic HTML canvas knowledge, I thought of creating my own solution. How hard could it be, right?

For my initial prototype, I started with plain HTML and JavaScript. As the project grew more complex, I switched to using Vite and TypeScript. If the project continues to expand, I may consider migrating to a more advanced framework like React or Vue.js. But for now, my focus is on creating a usable canvas.

Hey ChatGPT, can you help me?

I reached out to ChatGPT to help me with the starting point. With a few iterations, it provided a decent starting code that saved me about 4 hours. However, as things got more complicated, the prompt became challenging, and I decided to stick with the code I had. I refactored and continued the development myself.

I introduced fixes and new features, such as hover effects and expanding the dot grid based on user input. But as the plain HTML and JavaScript code grew messy and hard to read, I decided to rewrite it in TypeScript and separate the functionalities. Using Vite, I built my TypeScript code and refactored it, resulting in simpler and more flexible code.

Current Features:

In just one weekend, I aimed to create a usable minimum viable product (MVP) and implemented the following features:

  • Resize the dot grid
  • Dots
    • Add color
    • Add descriptions / delete
    • Move dot
    • Select dot
    • Hover dot - add effect, shows the whole description
  • Lines
    • Add color
    • Connect two dots
    • Delete lines
    • Select lines
    • Hover effect
  • Save the project as an image
  • Save the project to a file for sharing or future use
  • Save and load projects from local storage
  • ICs (show pin number and description) *incomplete
  • Reset the project
  • Undo / redo changes
  • Keyboard shortcuts

Check out the GitHub codebase or the live demo

Solving a Problem:

Choosing the right project to start can be challenging. The best learning project is one that solves your own problems. It's more enjoyable and satisfying to work on something that interests you and addresses your needs.

Future Plans:

  • Create a more user-friendly tools menu
  • Finish the IC feature
  • Add a component creator
  • Migrate to React or Vue

Contributing:

I'm not particularly skilled with CSS, so if you'd like to contribute (e.g. to the tools menu design), your help would be greatly appreciated.

In a future post, I may delve into the underlying math and solutions. Thank you for reading, have a great day! ❤️
item.author: maurerkrisztian

item.title: 提升您的开发者职业生涯...... | Levelling up your developer career....
item.description:
我最近（2023 年 7 月 7 日）与其他小组成员一起参与了围绕该主题的讨论。 这是围绕“让开发人员更快构建、更多创新”的大型活动的一部分。

如果您有兴趣观看，请查看录音。 如果没有，那也没关系！ 讨论围绕：

  • 开发者之旅
  • 开发人员的学习路径和机会
  • 深度专业化
  • 再培训，特别针对职业中期人员
  • 成为云原生开发者

只是想让你知道，这些讨论点（和我的观点）并不“新颖”。 其中大部分都是常识，我相信您以前听说过。 回顾一下没有什么坏处，不是吗？

我强调了一些非传统的选择，它们比完成课程或获得认证更具开放性和探索性。

首先是开源贡献：这对于职业生涯早期的开发人员来说可能听起来非常令人畏惧。 但这就是秘密所在——因为这并不容易，但它确实能让你与众不同。

多年来，我亲眼目睹了许多成功故事，包括学生和早期职业专业人士。

在在线论坛上回答问题：StackOverflow 是一个很好的例子（但不是唯一的例子！）只需选择您最喜欢的标签并过滤未回答的问题即可。 从小事开始，也许每周提出一两个问题。 一旦你坚持这样做并完成整个过程，你就会惊讶地发现你的调试和解决问题的技能如何提高。

文档：是的，请阅读文档！ 下次遇到该异常时，请阅读 API 文档。 也许您正在遵循教程但无法让它发挥作用。 不要直接进入搜索引擎，或者 StackOverflow（或者更糟糕的是，ChatGPT 🙄） 请深入研究官方文档。 一开始会很困难，但从长远来看，你会积累很多知识。 而且，也许如果文档及其开源中确实缺少某些内容，只需做出贡献并帮助其他开发人员🙌

了解某事和解释它（特别是通过书面形式）是两件不同的事情。 顺便说一句，作为一个普通的博主，我绝对对此有偏见（只是指出这一点！）

这些👆🏼都是隐藏在众目睽睽之下的资源和机会。 您不必同时选择所有这些。 缓慢而稳定地进行。 从一个开始，或者可能发现其他的。 我确信我还没有全部探索过。

我们实际上是从讨论“开发者之旅”开始的

我首先写了“学习”，因为这是一个我很关心的话题

嗯，我从五岁起就不是一个真正尝试摆弄计算机的极客，所以从这个意义上说，实际上，它开始时非常无聊。 但我很幸运能够经历各种各样的角色。 从一名工程师开始，致力于基于 Java 的中间件产品，对问题进行分类，解决各种操作系统、数据库和 LDAP 系统中的错误 - 那时还没有 Docker 🤷🏽‍♂️ 所以你可以想象它是什么样的！

然后，我继续在生产中为客户实施这些产品。 从那时起，我进军云和开源领域，并在产品管理和开发人员倡导之间来回切换。 如果我回顾迄今为止的旅程，我会回想起我职业生涯的开始和我所经历的“火的洗礼”。 这很艰难，相信我😅但它做了两件重要的事情：

  1. 迫使我深入了解大型系统，
  2. 有点让我快速学习新技术......

如果我要挑选一个要点，特别是对于职业生涯早期的人们来说，那就是揭穿“转变为架构师、项目经理或工程经理（或类似）角色，并保持技术水平”的神话或困惑。

同时保持亲力亲为非常重要（以我的愚见）。 当然，不同的人可能有不同的处理方式。 但是，总的来说，我认为不断构建东西、亲自动手并花时间在 IDE 中可以获得很多价值 ⌨️ 因为这才是真正的魔法发生的地方 🪄

不知道大家有没有听说过T型这个词？ 如果您有，请耐心等待并认为这是一个复习。

然而，他们在一两个领域也拥有深厚的专业知识，这可以通过“T”的垂直词干来表示。

但说起来容易做起来难，并且没有“一种正确的方法”。 我可以给大家举几个例子：

  • 例如，您可能是从事数据库工作的人，您可能专门研究 NoSQL，但对分布式系统概念或迁移等企业挑战有广泛的了解。
  • 同样，云工程师可能对如何使用各种云提供商实施解决方案拥有广泛的知识，但对 AWS 上的容器化服务（如 EKS、ECS、App Runner 等）有深入的专业知识。
  • 另一个典型的例子是 AWS 的专家解决方案架构师。 他们是各自领域的冠军，但同时他们也无法摆脱对 AWS 的广泛了解。

所以我想我想表达的观点是 -是的，绝对要追求深度专业化，但不是在所有领域 😂 同时，不要变成一个“万事通，一无是处”的角色。 尝试取得良好的平衡。 有一条细线，小心地踩。

持续学习几乎是我工作的重要组成部分，我真的很喜欢它并对此表示感谢！ 但是，我确实同意有时会有点累😞

曾经有一段时间，NoSQL 称霸。 然后，出现了 Kubernetes 浪潮（我认为现在已经稳定了）。 然后是别的东西——也许是 Web3？ 现在是ChatGPT，不知道接下来会怎样？ 所有这一切都会根据你与谁交谈而有所不同......

对于职业生涯中期的人来说，跟上忙碌的节奏可能很困难，我实际上建议坚持T 形策略。 如果您发现您的专业领域与这一新浪潮之间存在重叠，我建议您利用这一点并应用您已有的知识来驾驭新的领域。

几个个人例子，我通过将它们与以前的概念相结合来学习新东西：

  • 如何在 Kubernetes 上运行数据库等有状态系统。 或者在 Kubernetes 上运行大规模处理管道有哪些细微差别。
  • 我们如何看待这一领域的数据——这催生了矢量数据库等。

还有一件事。 它可能（真的）被低估了——那就是阅读大量内容。

我不是在谈论博客文章等。阅读涵盖您正在从事的领域的基础知识的高质量材料或书籍。 对我来说，这就像“设计数据密集型应用程序”。 目标是拥有真正坚实的基础。 它会让您为市场上的下一个“大事”做好准备，它总是指日可待 😉 💪

我实际上并没有谈论这些……但小组成员表达的观点与我想说的类似。 所以我想在这里补充我的两分钱。

请记住，您不再坐在本地服务器机房中（就像我一样！），或者不再与系统管理员交谈来启动一组服务器来部署您的软件。 在云中，您已经向上移动了堆栈。 现在是一个不同的抽象级别。 就像DevOps一样，云原生也是一种不同的思维方式。 作为云原生开发人员，您需要接受随之而来的新范例。

我实际上可以在这里想到几个方面：

微服务方法- 我个人不喜欢使用术语“微服务”（我喜欢将其视为分布式系统）。 无论您是否喜欢，或者您是否意识到，当您在云上进行构建时，您都是在一个庞大的分布式系统之上进行构建。

拥抱失败——系统应该设计为具有容错能力，具有自动恢复和回滚……以及所有好的东西。 而且...自动化- 如果您在云中并且仍在使用控制台进行 ClickOps 从开发到生产。 那么你还不是真正的云原生。

人们可以在这里谈论很多其他方面，特别是围绕可观察性和监控。 但这些绝对是我最关心的。

如果您考虑一下这些表现出来的具体领域：

  • 无服务器- 不仅仅是计算（如 AWS Lambda 或 Fargate），还有数据库（如 DynamoDB）。
  • 基础设施即代码- 所有基础设施都触手可及（实际上是 API 调用......）

对于刚开始云原生之旅的人（是的，总有新人！） - 不要陷入“无服务器等于功能”、“Kubernetes 是所有问题的解决方案”之类的神话和误用词的陷阱“等等。使用正确的工具完成正确的工作。

请记住，仅仅因为您从这次会议回来并学到了这个“很棒的新东西”，并不意味着您需要在第二天将其投入生产🤣

好了，又回到平常的帖子了！ 下次见👋🏼



原文:

I was recently (July 7, 2023) involved in a discussion around this topic along with other panelists. This was part of a larger event around Empowering Developers to Build Faster and Innovate More.

If you are interested in watching, please check out the recording. If not, that's ok! 😀 I think its worth sharing some insights in a blog post here since it's a topic close to my heart (Developer enablement) and it's a good change up from the usual stuff I usually write about.

Discussions were around:

  • Journey as a developer
  • Learning paths and opportunities for developers
  • Deep specialisation
  • Re-skilling, specially for mid-career folks
  • Being a cloud-native developer

Just so that you know, these discussion points (and my opinions) are not "novel". Most of it is common sense and I am sure you have heard it before. No harm in a recap, is it?

What about "learning paths and opportunities for developers"?

I highlighted a few non-traditional options which are more open-ended and exploratory as opposed to completing a course, or getting certified.

First is Open source contributions: This may sound very daunting for specially for developers early on in their career. But that's where the secret lies - because it's not easy, it can really set you apart.

I have personally witnessed many success stories over the years, including students and early career professionals.

Answering questions on online forums: StackOverflow is a great example (but not the only one!) Just pick your favorite tag and filter the unanswered questions. Start small, maybe one or two questions a week. Once you do this consistently and go through the process, you will be surprised by how your debugging and problem-solving skills improve.

Documentation: Yes, read the documentation! Next time you get that exception, go read the API documentation. Maybe you are following a tutorial and can't get it to work. Don't go directly a search engine, or StackOverflow (or worse, ChatGPT 🙄) Please dig into the official documentation. It will be hard at first, but a side effect you will build a lot of knowledge in the long term. And, maybe if something is really missing in the docs and its open source, just make that contribution and help other developers 🙌

...the last one is, Start blogging/writing - Because this will really force you to think. Knowing something and explaining that, specially in writing are two different things. By the way, as someone who is a regular blogger, I am definitely biased towards this (just calling that out!)

These 👆🏼 are all resources and opportunities hidden in plain sight. You don't have to pick all of these at the same time. Take it slow and steady. Start with one perhaps or maybe discover others. I am sure I haven't explored them all.

We actually started off by discussing our "Journey as a developer"

I wrote about "learning" first since it's a topic close to my heart

Well I wasn't really a geek trying to tinker with computers since I was five, so in that sense, it started out being quite boring, actually. But I have been lucky to have experienced a wide spectrum of roles. Started off as an engineer, working on Java based middleware products, triaging issues, solving bugs across a variety of operating systems and databases and LDAP systems - Docker wasn't a thing that time 🤷🏽‍♂️ So you can imagine how it was like!

Then, I moved on to implementing these products for customers, in production. From there on I forayed into the cloud and open-source space and switched back and forth between Product Management and Developer Advocacy. And if I reflect upon my journey so far, I think back to the beginning of my career and the "baptism by fire" that I had. It was tough, trust me 😅 But it did two important things:

  1. Forced me to understand large systems in and out,
  2. And sort of wired me up to learn new technologies rapidly...

If I were to pick one take-away, specially for folks early on in their career, it would be debunking this myth or confusion around "transitioning into an architect or a PM or engineering manager (or similar) role, and remaining technical".

It's really important (in my humble opinion) to stay hands-on at the same time. Sure, different individuals might have different ways of going about it. But, overall I think there is a lot of value to be gained from always building things and getting your hands dirty and spending time in your IDE ⌨️ Because that's where the real magic happens 🪄

... and then it was about "deep specialization" for developers

I am not sure if folks have heard of the term T-shaped? If you have, please bear with me and consider this a refresher.

A T-shaped developer is someone with broad knowledge across many aspects of software development represented by the horizontal bar of the 'T'. Yet, they also have deep expertise in one or two areas, signified by the vertical stem of the 'T'.

But it is easier said than done and there is no "one right way". I can cite a few examples for you:

  • For example, you could be someone who works on databases, you could specialize in NoSQL, but have a broad knowledge of distributed systems concepts, or enterprise challenges like migration etc.
  • Similarly a Cloud Engineer could have a broad knowledge of how to implement solutions using various cloud providers, but have deep specialty with containerized services on AWS like EKS, ECS, App Runner etc.
  • Another classic example is Specialist Solution Architects at AWS. They are champions in their respective areas, but at the same time they cannot get away with not having a broad knowledge of AWS.

So I guess the point I am trying to make is - Yes, absolutely go for deep specialization, but not in everything 😂 At the same time, don't turn into a "jack of all trades, master of none" persona. Try and strike a good balance. There is a fine line, tread it carefully.

How about "re-skilling, specially for mid-career folks"?

Continuous learning pretty much been part and parcel of my job, I really enjoy it and thankful for it! But, I do agree that it can be a bit exhausting at times 😞

There was a time when NoSQL ruled the roost. Then, there was this Kubernetes wave that was happening (which i think is kind of stable now). And then it was something else - Web3 maybe? Now it's ChatGPT, and I don't know what's next? And all this will be different depending on who you are talking to...

For mid-career folks, it can be a tricky to keep up with the hectic pace, and I would actually recommend sticking to the T-shaped strategy. If you see an overlap between your expertise area and this new wave, I advise using that to your advantage and applying what you already know in order to navigate a new landscape.

Couple of personal examples, where I learned new stuff by marrying them with previous concepts:

  • How to run stateful systems like databases on Kubernetes. Or what are the nuances of running large scale processing pipelines on Kubernetes.
  • ... and more recently is in the context of Generative AI. How do we think about data in this area - this has given rise to Vector Databases and so on.

And there is one more thing. It's probably (really) underrated - and that is reading a lot.

And I am not talking about blog posts etc. Read high quality material or books that cover fundamentals of areas you are working on. For me it would be something like "Designing Data Intensive Applications". The goal is to have really strong fundamentals. It will keep you ready for the next "big thing" in the market, which is always around the corner 😉 💪

"Being a cloud-native developer" - How is it different?

I didn't actually talk about these... but the opinions expressed by the panelists were similar to what I would have said. So I am just going to add my two cents here.

(probably) Not a new topic in 2023, but an interesting one, having worked on helping ship such platforms in the past.

Remember that you are not sitting in your on-premise server room anymore (like I was, literally!), or not talking to system admins to bring up a fleet of servers to deploy your software. In the cloud, you have moved up the stack. It's a different abstraction level now. Just like DevOps, cloud-native is also a different way of thinking. And being a cloud-native developer, you need to embrace the new paradigms that come along with it.

I can actually think of few areas here:

Microservices approach - I personally don't like using the term "microserivces" (I like to think of it as distributed systems). Whether you like it or not, or you realize it or not, when you are building on the cloud, you are building on top of a massive distributed system.

...and that brings up the point about

Embracing failure - Systems should be designed to be fault-tolerant, with automated recovery and rollbacks... and all the good stuff. "Failures are a given and everything will eventually fail over time" - enough said!

And... automation - If you are in the cloud and still doing ClickOps using the console to go from dev to production. Well then you aren't really cloud native.

And one could talk about so many other aspects here, specially around observability and monitoring. But these are definitely top of mind for me.

If you think of concrete areas where these have manifested:

  • Docker - Packaging your apps as containers and running them on compute such as Kubernetes, or another PaaS (or CaaS?).
  • Serverless - It's not just the compute (like AWS Lambda or Fargate), but also databases (like DynamoDB).
  • Infrastructure as code - All infra is available at tip of your fingertips (literally an API call away...)

For folks that are new to their cloud native journey (yes, there is always someone who is new!) - don't fall prey to myths and misnomers like "Serverless is equal to functions", "Kubernetes is the solution to all the problems" etc. Use the right tool for the right job.

And remember, just because you came back from this conference and learned this "awesome new thing", doesn't mean you need to put it into production the very next day 🤣

Alright, back to the usual posts again! See you next time 👋🏼
item.author: abhirockzz

item.title: MongoDB 副本集的最佳 DX | Best DX for MongoDB Replica set
item.description:
西贝柳斯·塞拉菲尼 (Sibelius Seraphini)为Woovi 创作的作品

发表于 7 月 10 日

我们在生产中运行 MongoDB 副本集，因为这是冗余和数据可用性的推荐设置。 我们的 MongoDB 节点是 Kubernetes 中具有持久卷的 pod，因此 pod 可以重新启动而不会丢失任何数据。

我们的副本集上还有 1 个隐藏成员，用于备份和元数据库，因此它不会影响生产工作流程。 隐藏成员不接受来自副本集连接的任何读取。

有些功能仅在副本集模式下运行 MongoDB 时可用，例如事务和变更流。 发生这种情况是因为这些功能需要访问仅在副本集配置中可用的oplog 。

我们正在使用变更流来使我们的架构更加事件驱动，并更新我们的 Elasticsearch 索引来支持我们的 Woovi 搜索功能。 我们将在下一篇博客文章中深入探讨我们的大规模搜索架构。

我们非常注重 DX（开发人员体验），因此运行 MongoDB 副本集对于我们团队中的每个人来说都应该是简单快速的，让我们看看我们如何实现这一目标。

使用 Docker for Mac 运行 docker 又重又慢，我们切换到OrbStack ，感觉我们甚至不再运行 docker，它感觉是原生的。

为了运行最小的 MongoDB 副本集，我们决定运行 2 个 mongodb 节点，docker compose 使得设置变得非常容易

version: '3.8' services: mongo1: container_name: mongo1 image: mongo:latest command: --bind_ip_all --replSet rs0 ports: - '28017:27017' volumes: - mongodb_data_container:/data/mongod1db - ./scripts/mongodb/rs-init.sh:/scripts/rs-init.sh networks: mongo-network: aliases: - mongo1.localhost ipv4_address: 172.20.0.2 links: - mongo2 mongo2: container_name: mongo2 image: mongo:latest command: --bind_ip_all --replSet rs0 ports: - '29017:27017' volumes: - mongodb_data_container:/data/mongod2db networks: mongo-network: aliases: - mongo2.localhost ipv4_address: 172.20.0.3 volumes: mongodb_data_container: cache: driver: local networks: mongo-network: name: mongo-network driver: bridge ipam: config: - subnet: 172.20.0.0/16 gateway: 172.20.0.254

让我们深入研究这个 docker compose 文件。

我们声明了 2 个服务：mongo1 和 mongo2。 它们中的每一个都映射到 localhost 中的不同端口，28017 和 29017。它们中的每一个在自己的卷中都有自己的数据

它们每个都有固定的IP 172.20.0.2和172.20.0.3，固定的IP对于使mongodb驱动正确解析mongo1和mongo2很重要。

我们还需要一个脚本来配置副本集

该脚本将启动 mongosh (mongo cli shell)，并配置副本集成员。
由于它们位于 docker 内的同一网络中，因此它们可以使用名称 mongo1 和 mongo2 找到彼此。

我们完成此设置的最终脚本只需要一个命令： mongodb-rs.sh

 docker-compose up -d sleep 5 docker exec mongo1 ./scripts/rs-init.sh

它将启动 docker compose，等待 5 秒并配置副本集。

连接 URI 会是这样的

mongodb://mongo1:27017,mongo2:27017/db?replicaSet=rs0

但是您的主机无法解析mongo1或mongo2 ，因此我们需要“模拟”DNS。

我们有这个命令将固定IP添加到我们的/etc/hosts中，这样我们的主机就可以正确解析mongo1 。

 sudo -- sh -c "echo 172.20.0.2 mongo1 >> /etc/hosts" sudo -- sh -c "echo 172.20.0.3 mongo2 >> /etc/hosts"

综上所述

这听起来工作量很大，确实如此。 我们需要一些时间才能找到适合每个人的完美配置，但这是值得的。

我们降低了团队中每个人的设置成本，避免浪费大量时间。

在 Woovi，我们在构建模块上投入了大量资金，这将降低我们更快地将新代码交付到生产环境的成本。
设置 MongoDB 副本集环境的 1 个命令是我们这项投资的示例之一。

扩展不仅仅是在生产中向 Kubernetes 添加更多 Pod，还在于降低在现有软件之上开发新软件的成本。

伍维
为了实现这一点，Woovi 为商家提供即时支付解决方案来接受订单。

如果您想与我们合作，我们正在招聘！

弗朗西斯科·丘拉 - 4 月 14 日

一旦被暂停，woovi 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到其暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，woovi 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，woovi 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己才能访问。

如果 woovi 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从仪表板重新发布帖子。

笔记：

感谢您保证 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以执行以下操作来标记 woovi：

使 woovi 的所有帖子不那么明显

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 woovi 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Sibelius Seraphini for Woovi

Posted on Jul 10

 

We run a MongoDB replica set in production as this is the recommended setup for redundancy and data availability. We MongoDB node is a pod in our Kubernetes with persistent volumes, so the pod can restart without losing any data.

We also have 1 hidden member on our replica set that is used for backup and Metabase, so it does not affect production workflow. A hidden member does not accept any reads from replica set connection.

There are some features that are only available when running MongoDB in replica set mode like transactions and change streams. This happens because these features require to access the oplog only available in replica set config.

We are using change streams to make our architecture more event driven and to update our Elasticsearch index to power our Woovi search feature. We are going to write in the next blog post the deep dive of our search architecture at scale.

We focus a lot on DX (developer experience), so running a MongoDB replica set should be easy and fast for everyone in our team, let's see how we achieve that.

MongoDB Docker Compose Setup

Everybody at Woovi has a macbook m1 to make sure they are productive.
Running docker using Docker for Mac was heavy and slow, we switched to OrbStack and it feels that we are not even running docker anymore, it feels native.

To run a minimal MongoDB Replica set we decided to run 2 mongodb nodes, docker compose makes it very easy to setup

version: '3.8'
services: mongo1: container_name: mongo1 image: mongo:latest command: --bind_ip_all --replSet rs0 ports: - '28017:27017' volumes: - mongodb_data_container:/data/mongod1db - ./scripts/mongodb/rs-init.sh:/scripts/rs-init.sh networks: mongo-network: aliases: - mongo1.localhost ipv4_address: 172.20.0.2 links: - mongo2 mongo2: container_name: mongo2 image: mongo:latest command: --bind_ip_all --replSet rs0 ports: - '29017:27017' volumes: - mongodb_data_container:/data/mongod2db networks: mongo-network: aliases: - mongo2.localhost ipv4_address: 172.20.0.3 volumes: mongodb_data_container: cache: driver: local networks: mongo-network: name: mongo-network driver: bridge ipam: config: - subnet: 172.20.0.0/16 gateway: 172.20.0.254

Let's deep dive on this docker compose file.

We declared 2 services: mongo1, and mongo2. Each of them are mapped to different ports in localhost, 28017 and 29017. Each of them have their own data in their own volumes

Each of them have a fixed IP 172.20.0.2 and 172.20.0.3, fixed IP is important to make mongodb drive to resolve mongo1 and mongo2 properly.

We also need a script to configure the replica set

rs-init.sh

#!/bin/bash mongosh <<EOF
const config = { "_id": "rs0", "version": 1, "members": [ { "_id": 1, "host": "mongo1:27017", "priority": 2 }, { "_id": 2, "host": "mongo2:27017", "priority": 1 }, ]
};
rs.initiate(config, { force: true });
rs.status();
EOF

This script will start mongosh (mongo cli shell), and configure the replica set members.
As they are in the same network inside docker, they can find each other using their names, mongo1 and mongo2.

Our final script to make this setup only need one command is mongodb-rs.sh

docker-compose up -d sleep 5 docker exec mongo1 ./scripts/rs-init.sh

It will start the docker compose, it will wait for 5 seconds and it will configure the replica set.

The connect URI would be like this

mongodb://mongo1:27017,mongo2:27017/db?replicaSet=rs0

However your host machine can't resolve mongo1 or mongo2, so we need to "simulate" the DNS.

We have this command to add the fixed IP to our /etc/hosts, so our host machine can resolve mongo1 properly.

sudo -- sh -c "echo 172.20.0.2 mongo1 >> /etc/hosts"
sudo -- sh -c "echo 172.20.0.3 mongo2 >> /etc/hosts"

In Conclusion

This sounds like a lot of work, and it is. It takes us some time to find the perfect configuration that works well for everybody, but it is worth it.

As we reduced the cost of the setup to everyone in the team avoiding wasting a lot of time.

At Woovi we invest a lot in building blocks that will reduce the cost of us shipping new code faster to production.
The 1 command to setup a MongoDB replica set environment is one of our example of this investment.

Scaling in not only adding more pods to your Kubernetes in production, it is all about reducing the cost of developing new software on top of the existing one.

Woovi
Woovi is a Startup that enables shoppers to pay as they like. To make this possible, Woovi provides instant payment solutions for merchants to accept orders.

If you want to work with us, we are hiring!

Francesco Ciulla - Apr 14

Once suspended, woovi will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, woovi will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by woovi will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If woovi is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Note:

Thanks for keeping DEV Community safe. Here is what you can do to flag woovi:

Make all posts by woovi less visible

woovi consistently posts content that violates DEV Community's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging woovi will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: sibelius

item.title: 使用 ChatGPT 创建更好的 StackOverflow | Creating a Better StackOverflow with ChatGPT
item.description:
现在您可能已经听说过OpenAI 关闭 ChatGPT 上的互联网搜索的消息。 在 AINIRO，我们相信软件进化本身就是一个更高的目标，因此我们创建了AINIRO Oracle，这是一个使用 ChatGPT 来回答问题的搜索引擎。

要理解我们的动机，请想象以下用例，您希望在其中找到以下问题的答案：

美国第35任总统的妻子是谁？

使用传统搜索引擎寻找上述问题的答案的过程往往极其复杂，需要进行大量不必要的手动工作。

  1. 找出谁是第 35 任总统。 这通常包括找到所有总统，然后仔细浏览此列表，直到找到第 35 位。
  2. 查找第 35 任总统的维基百科文章，然后扫描以查找他的妻子。

上面的问题很简单，但更复杂的问题很容易占用您 10 到 20 分钟的时间。 使用我们的Oracle 引擎，该过程只需 30 秒，并且 100% 自动。 这是问题的答案： 谁是美国第35任总统的妻子。 下面是它根据指定问题自动生成的另一篇示例文章。

显然，自从我们昨天发布它以来，我们只玩了几天。 但总的来说，我想说这“感觉”比 WikiPedia、StackOverflow 和 Google 好一百万倍。 无需滚动浏览无数不相关内容的数据来找到问题的答案，也无需在 Google 中点击 5 个链接才能弹出相关文章。 如果您是一名软件开发人员，在使用了一段时间后您可能会体会到以下内容......

它相当于 StackOverflow 的 AI 版本

预言机如何工作？

首先，它接受您的查询并将其发送到DuckDuckGo 。 然后它并行抓取前 5 个结果，并创建一个上下文。 此上下文将发送到ChatGPT ，并附有回答原始问题的说明。 由于强大的网络抓取工具与一些智能提示工程相结合，我们能够确保它始终将您的问题的答案放在生成的文章的顶部，同时在下面添加其他相关内容。

结果成为一个搜索引擎，或者一个 Oracle 应答机，它“剔除废话” ，并在顶部回答您的问题，并将其答案中最重要的部分放在顶部 - 显着简化了回答您的问题的任务。

我们的 Oracle 引擎和 ChatGPT 之间的一个关键区别是，除了实时数据之外，我们的 Oracle 引擎可以说可以访问“数亿倍的数据” 。 如果您正在搜索与当代信息相关的信息，例如“谁赢得了 2023 年美国公开赛”，那么最后一点很重要。 如果你问 ChatGPT 谁赢得了美国公开赛，它会不知道。 如果您询问我们的 Oracle 引擎谁赢得了 2023 年美国公开赛，它会为您提供完美的答案，通常会添加与您的答案相关的图像。

更好的 StackOverflow

声称在 2 天内发明了更好的 StackOveflow 是一个相当有力的声明。 不过，我将通过在 Google 上对 StackOverflow 的 3 个关键字进行站点搜索来证明这一点，然后重现对我们的 Oracle Machine 的相同查询。 我在 StackOverflow 上的所有搜索都是通过“站点搜索”完成的，我选择了 Google 可以找到的第一篇文章。

堆栈溢出

神谕机

除此之外，它没有足够大的上下文来列出所有 23 种设计模式，我想说我们的 Oracle Machine 可以更好地回答上述三个问题中的至少两个。

社交媒体争论机

除了上述明显的用例，可以极其快速且极其准确地找到问题的答案之外，它还被称为“社交媒体应答机” ，这意味着如果有人在某些社交媒体环境中问你任何问题，你可以将其写成向我们的 Oracle Machine 提出问题，并在几秒钟内提供该问题的高度准确的答案。

此外，它还创建了一篇永久的高质量文章，您可以通过电子邮件分享和发送给其他人，用作 Facebook 评论、Twitter 回复等。Oracle 机器还将缓存其答案，这样，如果另一个用户提出的问题超过与之前回答过的问题相似度达到 95%，它将返回此答案而不是生成新答案。 然而，这只适用于 5 天，否则随着时间的推移它会变得陈旧，无法回答有关最近事件的问题。

所有这些特征使其（我希望）成为您的互联网工具的非常有价值的补充，提供某种独特的价值主张，总体上简化您的生活。

包起来

根据记录，我们是一家人工智能专家软件开发公司，专门专注于人工智能和机器学习。 我们正在帮助小型和大型公司拥抱人工智能和自动化。 如果您有兴趣与我们讨论我们的ChatGPT 聊天机器人、我们的AI 网站搜索产品​​或任何与 AI 相关的需求 - 我们很乐意听取您的意见。

证据就在布丁里。 下面是我向我们的 Oracle 系统提出的一个问题。 我想你可以体会到它是如何围绕 StackOverflow 运行的:)



原文:

By now you've probably heard the news about OpenAI turning off internet search on ChatGPT. At AINIRO we believe that software evolution by itself is a higher purpose, so we created AINIRO Oracle, the Search Engine that uses ChatGPT to answer questions.

To understand our motivation, imagine the following use case where you want to find the answer to the following question:

Who was the 35th President of the US' wife?

The process of finding an answer to the above question with a traditional search engine tends to be ridiculously complex with a lot of manual unnecessary work.

  1. Find out who was the 35th President. This typically including finding all Presidents, for then to wade through this list until you're at number 35.
  2. Find the WikiPedia article for the 35th President, for then to scan it for his wife.

The above question is a simple one, but more complex questions can easily take up 10 to 20 minutes of your time. With our Oracle engine the process takes 30 seconds, and is 100% automatic. Here's the answer to the question: Who Was The 35th President Of The US' Wife. Below is another example article it automatically produced given the specified question.

We've only played with it for a couple of days, obviously, since we released it yesterday. But in general, I'd say this "feels" one million times better than WikiPedia, StackOverflow, and Google. No need to scroll through endless data of irrelevant content to find the answer to your question, and no need to click 5 links in Google before a relevant article pops up. If you're a software developer you can probably relate to the following after having played with it for a while ...

It's the AI equivalent of StackOverflow

How the Oracle Machine works?

First it takes your query and sends it to DuckDuckGo. Then it scrapes the top 5 results in parallel, and creates a context. This context is sent to ChatGPT with instructions to answer the original question. Due to a kick ass web scraper, combined with some intelligent prompt engineering, we're able to make sure it always puts the answer to your question at the top of the generated article, while adding additional relevant content further down.

The result becomes a search engine, or an Oracle answering machine, that "cuts through the crap", and answers your questions with the most significant parts of its answer at the top - Significantly simplifying the task of having your questions answered.

One crucial difference between our Oracle engine and ChatGPT, is that our Oracle engine arguably has access to "a bajillion times as much data", in addition to of course real time data. The last point is kind of important if you're searching for information related to contemporary information such as for instance "Who Won US Open in 2023". If you ask ChatGPT who won US Open it will have no idea. If you ask our Oracle engine who won US Open in 2023, it will provide a perfect answer for you, often adding images that are relevant to your answer.

The Better StackOverflow

Claiming to have invented a better StackOveflow in 2 days is a pretty strong statement. However, I will prove it by doing a site search on Google for 3 keywords towards StackOverflow, for then to reproduce the same queries towards our Oracle Machine. All my searches at StackOverflow are done with a "site search" and I chose the first article Google could find.

StackOverflow

Oracle Machine

Besides from that it didn't have a large enough context to list all 23 design patterns, I'd say at least two of the above three questions are far better answered by our Oracle Machine.

Social Media Argument Machine

In addition to the above obvious use cases for finding answers to questions extremely fast and extremely accurate, it also seconds as "a social media answer machine", implying if somebody asks you about anything in some social media context, you can write it out as a question to our Oracle Machine, and provide a highly accurate answer to the question in some few seconds.

The Oracle Machine will do its best to try to preserve its sources, and also allow you to reproduce its DuckDuckGo query. In addition it creates a permanent high quality article you can share and send to others on email, use as Facebook comments, Twitter replies, etc. The Oracle Machine will also cache its answers, such that if another user asks a questions that has more than 95% similarity with a question it has previously answered, it will return this answer instead of generating a new answer. However, this is only true for 5 days, since otherwise it would become stale over time, incapable of answering questions about recent events.

All of these traits makes it (I hope) a highly valuable addition to your internet tooling, delivering a somewhat unique value proposition, simplifying your life in general.

Wrapping up

For the record, we're an AI expert software development company, focusing exclusively on AI and Machine Learning. We're helping small and large companies to embrace AI and automation. If you're interested in talking to us about our ChatGPT chatbot, our AI Website Search product, or whatever needs you've got related to AI - We would love to hear from you.

The proof is in the pudding. Below is a question I phrased to our Oracle system. I think you can appreciate how it runs in circles around StackOverflow :)
item.author: polterguy

item.title: 探索以太坊钱包：综合指南 | Exploring Ethereum Wallets: A Comprehensive Guide
item.description:
以太坊钱包是允许用户与以太坊交互的基本软件应用程序。 它们提供对您资金的访问、处理密钥管理、跟踪余额并支持交易的创建和签署。 有些钱包甚至支持与智能合约和代币的交互。

从程序员的角度来看，钱包特指负责存储和管理用户密钥的系统。 虽然某些钱包只专注于密钥管理，但其他钱包属于更广泛的浏览器类别，它们充当基于以太坊的去中心化应用程序（DApp）的界面。

在本章中，我们将主要探讨作为私钥容器的钱包以及旨在管理这些密钥的系统。

钱包技术概述

在设计以太坊钱包时，在便利性和隐私性之间找到平衡非常重要。 使用单一密钥处理所有事务很容易，但会损害隐私，因为所有交易都可以追踪。 另一方面，为每笔交易使用新密钥更加私密，但难以管理。 好的钱包设计旨在达到适当的平衡。

与普遍的看法相反，以太坊钱包只存储密钥，而不存储实际的以太币或代币。 代币记录在以太坊区块链上，用户通过使用钱包密钥签署交易来控制它们。 将以太坊钱包视为钥匙串。 尽管存在技术上的区别，但在实践中并不产生重大影响。 然而，从集中式银行思维转向去中心化的区块链系统非常重要，在这种系统中，任何人都可以看到余额，但需要获得许可才能发起交易。

以太坊钱包旨在保存加密密钥，而不是直接存储以太币或代币。 这些钱包可以与钥匙串进行比较，因为它们包含成对的私钥和公钥。 通过使用他们的私钥，用户可以签署交易，提供他们对以太币的所有权的证据。 以太币的实际存储发生在以太坊区块链上。

钱包主要有两种类型：非确定性钱包和确定性钱包。

  • 非确定性钱包，也称为 JBOK 钱包，从随机数生成独立密钥。 这些键彼此不相关。
  • 确定性钱包从称为种子的单个主密钥中派生出所有密钥。 这意味着确定性钱包中的所有密钥都已连接，并且如果您有原始种子，则可以重新生成。 分层确定性钱包（BIP-32/BIP-44）使用常用的树状结构来派生密钥。

为了增强确定性钱包的安全性，种子通常被转换为称为助记码字的单词列表。 应写下此列表并将其保存在安全的地方，因为如果需要，它可用于重新创建钱包。 然而，保护助记码至关重要，因为任何有权访问它们的人都可以控制钱包及其相关的以太币和智能合约。 避免以电子方式存储恢复词，而是将其写在纸上，安全地存储。

在以下部分中，我们将更详细地探讨这些钱包技术。 请记住优先考虑助记词的安全性！

不确定性（随机）钱包

第一个以太坊钱包在预售期间使用，在每个钱包文件中存储一个随机生成的私钥。 然而，这些“旧式”钱包正在被确定性钱包所取代，确定性钱包在很多方面都被认为是优越的。 建议避免重复使用以太坊地址以最大限度地保护隐私。 这意味着每次收到资金时都使用新地址（带有新私钥） 。 有些人甚至为每笔交易使用一个新地址，尽管在处理代币时这可能会变得昂贵。

在非确定性钱包中，您需要定期增加密钥列表并进行备份。 如果您在备份钱包之前丢失了数据，您将无法访问您的资金和智能合约。 许多以太坊客户端（例如geth ）使用密钥库文件。 该文件采用 JSON 编码，包含一个随机生成的私钥，并使用密码进行加密以提高安全性。

 { "address": "001d3f1ef827552ae1114027bd3ecf1f086ba0f9", "crypto": { "cipher": "aes-128-ctr", "ciphertext": "233a9f4d236ed0c13394b504b6da5df02587c8bf1ad8946f6f2b58f055507ece", "cipherparams": { "iv": "d10c6ec5bae81b6cb9144de81037fa15" }, "kdf": "scrypt", "kdfparams": { "dklen": 32, "n": 262144, "p": 1, "r": 8, "salt": "99d37a47c7c9429c66976f643f386a61b78b97f3246adca89abe4245d2788407" }, "mac": "594c8df1c8ee0ded8255a50caf07e8c12061fd859f4b7c76ab704b17c957e842" }, "id": "4fcb2ba4-ccdb-424f-89d5-26cce304bf9c", "version": 3 }

密钥库格式采用密钥派生函数 (KDF) ，也称为密码拉伸算法。 它可以防止暴力破解、字典攻击和彩虹表攻击。 该密码被重复哈希了 262,144 轮，使攻击者很难猜测该密码。

多个软件库（例如keythereum ）可以读取和写入密钥库格式。

确定性（种子）钱包

确定性钱包，也称为种子钱包，是具有从单个主密钥或种子派生的私钥的钱包。 种子是与其他数据（例如索引号或链码）组合的随机生成的数字。 对于确定性钱包，仅种子就足以恢复所有派生密钥。 这意味着在钱包创建过程中进行的一次备份足以保护钱包内的所有资金和智能合约。 种子还可以用于钱包导出或导入，从而可以轻松地在不同钱包系统之间传输密钥。

种子的安全性至关重要，因为访问种子就意味着可以完全访问整个钱包。 然而，将安全工作集中在单个数据上可能是有利的。

分层确定性钱包（BIP-32/BIP-44）

创建确定性钱包是为了更轻松地从单个种子生成多个密钥。 最先进的类型称为分层确定性（HD）钱包。 在HD钱包中，密钥以树形结构组织，父密钥派生子密钥，子密钥又可以派生孙密钥，依此类推。 这种结构可以更好地组织和管理密钥。
